event: Stone Age hunter-gatherers using Acheulean and later Middle Stone Age tools inhabit the river valleys and plateaus of present-day Angola.
Khoisan-speaking hunter-gatherer peoples become widespread across the region, leaving rock art and microlithic tool traditions.
Bantu-speaking agriculturalists begin migrating into the area, introducing ironworking, farming, and cattle-herding that gradually displace the Khoisan.
Bantu farming communities establish settled villages across Angola, developing pottery, metallurgy, and trade networks.
The Kingdom of Kongo coalesces under its founder Lukeni lua Nimi, becoming a powerful centralized state north of the Congo River with influence over northern Angola.
Portuguese explorer Diogo Cao reaches the mouth of the Congo River, initiating contact between the Kingdom of Kongo and Portugal.
King Nzinga a Nkuwu of Kongo is baptized as Joao I, and Christianity begins spreading among the Kongo elite.
Afonso I (Nzinga Mbemba) ascends the Kongo throne and promotes Christianity, literacy, and diplomatic ties with Portugal.
King Afonso I protests to the Portuguese crown over the disruptive escalation of the Atlantic slave trade in his kingdom.
Paulo Dias de Novais founds Luanda (Sao Paulo de Loanda), establishing the first permanent Portuguese colonial settlement and base for slave trading.
The Portuguese found Benguela on the central coast, expanding their colonial and slave-trading foothold southward.
Queen Nzinga (Njinga) of Ndongo and Matamba begins her decades-long resistance against Portuguese colonization and slaving.
Dutch forces capture Luanda, briefly seizing the Portuguese slave-trading center during the Dutch-Portuguese War.
A Brazilian-Portuguese expedition led by Salvador Correia de Sa recaptures Luanda from the Dutch.
Portuguese forces defeat the Kingdom of Kongo at the Battle of Mbwila, killing King Antonio I and hastening the kingdom's fragmentation.
The Portuguese subdue the Kingdom of Ndongo, consolidating control over much of central-western Angola.
Portugal officially abolishes the transatlantic slave trade in its colonies, though clandestine and internal slaving persists for decades.
The Berlin Conference recognizes Portuguese claims to Angola, formalizing the colony's place in the European Scramble for Africa.
The British Ultimatum forces Portugal to abandon its 'Pink Map' ambition of linking Angola and Mozambique across central Africa.
The Bailundo Revolt erupts among the Ovimbundu against Portuguese forced labor and trade abuses, and is brutally suppressed.
A military coup in Portugal ushers in authoritarian rule, tightening colonial control and exploitation in Angola.
Portugal redesignates Angola as an 'overseas province' to deflect international pressure for decolonization.
The People's Movement for the Liberation of Angola (MPLA) is founded, led by figures including Agostinho Neto.
The Angolan War of Independence begins with uprisings in Luanda and a major revolt in the north.
Jonas Savimbi founds the National Union for the Total Independence of Angola (UNITA), a third major liberation movement.
The Carnation Revolution overthrows Portugal's dictatorship, opening the way for Angolan decolonization.
Angola gains independence from Portugal on November 11, but rival movements MPLA, FNLA, and UNITA immediately plunge the country into civil war.
Angola is admitted to the United Nations and the Organisation of African Unity as the MPLA government consolidates power with Cuban and Soviet support.
President Agostinho Neto dies and is succeeded by Jose Eduardo dos Santos, who would rule Angola for nearly four decades.
The Battle of Cuito Cuanavale begins, a pivotal confrontation drawing in Cuban, Angolan, and South African forces.
The Bicesse Accords are signed, establishing a ceasefire and multiparty democracy and ending the Cold War proxy phase of the war.
Angola holds its first multiparty elections, but UNITA rejects the results and the civil war resumes with renewed intensity.
The Lusaka Protocol is signed in an attempt to end the fighting between the MPLA government and UNITA.
UNITA leader Jonas Savimbi is killed in combat, leading to a ceasefire that finally ends Angola's 27-year civil war.
A peace agreement with separatists in the oil-rich Cabinda enclave eases the long-running low-level conflict there.
Angola holds its first legislative elections since 1992, with the ruling MPLA winning an overwhelming majority.
A new constitution is adopted, abolishing direct presidential elections and concentrating executive power in the presidency.
Angola becomes one of Africa's leading oil producers, with petroleum exports driving rapid economic growth and reconstruction.
Joao Lourenco succeeds Jose Eduardo dos Santos as president, beginning an anti-corruption campaign and economic reforms.
The MPLA wins disputed general elections, returning Joao Lourenco to the presidency amid economic strain and falling oil revenues.
Former president Jose Eduardo dos Santos's death the prior year and ongoing corruption probes mark a continued reckoning with the dos Santos era.
Angola advances the Lobito Corridor railway project, positioning itself as a key route for exporting Central African minerals to global markets.
